questions.question: Consider the Fractional Knapsack problem with capacity W=15 and the following items: Item A (weight=10, value=60), Item B (weight=5, value=50), Item C (weight=8, value=45). Using the greedy approach based on value-to-weight ratio, determine which items are selected and the total value obtained. Also prove formally why the greedy approach yields an optimal solution for the Fractional Knapsack problem but NOT for the 0/1 Knapsack problem.
keyPoints: Calculate value/weight ratios: A=6, B=10, C=5.625
Select item B first (highest ratio), then item A (next highest)
Proof requires: exchange argument showing any optimal solution can be transformed into greedy solution
0/1 Knapsack fails because items cannot be divided - greedy may leave capacity unused

questions.question: Given the following set of activities with their start and finish times: S1(1,4), S2(3,5), S3(0,6), S4(5,7), S5(3,9), S6(5,9), S7(6,10), S8(8,11). Apply the Activity-Selection algorithm to find the maximum set of mutually compatible activities. Show the step-by-step execution and prove the correctness of the greedy choice property used.
keyPoints: Greedy choice: always select first finishing activity compatible with previously selected
Selected: S1, S2, S4, S7, S8 (5 activities)
Correctness proof: exchange argument showing optimal solution contains activity with earliest finish time
Show that removing first activity from optimal and replacing with greedy choice maintains optimality

questions.question: Using Kruskal's Algorithm, find the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) for the following weighted graph with vertices {A,B,C,D,E,F} and edges: AB(4), AC(3), AD(7), BC(2), BD(6), BE(5), CD(4), CE(8), DE(9), DF(4), EF(2). Show the step-by-step execution with union-find operations and calculate the total weight of the MST. Also explain why Kruskal's algorithm works correctly.
keyPoints: Sort edges by weight: BE(5), EF(2), BC(2), CD(4), DF(4), AB(4), AD(7), BD(6), CE(8), AC(3), DE(9)
Process: EF(2)→add, BC(2)→add, AC(3)→add, CD(4)→forms cycle-skip, DF(4)→add, AB(4)→add, BD(6)→add, CE(8)→add (6 edges now)
MST edges: EF, BC, AC, DF, AB, BD with total weight = 2+2+3+4+4+6 = 21
Union-Find: path compression and union by rank used
Correctness: cut property - for any cut, lightest edge crossing cut belongs to some MST

questions.question: For the character frequency table: A:45, B:13, C:12, D:16, E:9, F:5, construct the Huffman coding tree and determine the binary codewords for each character. Calculate the average code length and the total bits saved compared to fixed-length coding. Prove the optimality of Huffman coding using the exchange argument.
keyPoints: Build Huffman tree: combine F(5)+E(9)=14, combine C(12)+D(16)=28, combine 14+13=27, combine 27+28=55, combine 45+55=100
Codewords: A=0, B=1010, C=1000, D=1001, E=1011, F=1100 (varies based on tree construction)
Average length = Σ(freq/total × codeword length)
Fixed-length needs log2(6)=3 bits per character = 3×100 = 300 bits
Optimality proof: show any optimal prefix code satisfies sibling property and can be transformed via exchange of two largest frequency leaves
